name: varlock-claude-skill
description: Secure environment variable management ensuring secrets are never exposed in Claude sessions, terminals, logs, or git commits
capabilities: Provides secure patterns for loading environment variables without exposing values in chat
Guides proper .env file configuration and gitignore setup
Teaches safe methods to reference secrets in AI-assisted coding sessions
Explains how to avoid logging sensitive values in terminal output
Demonstrates commit-safe workflows that prevent secret leaks to version control
author: sickn33
securitySummary: This skill is documentation-only providing guidance on secure environment variable handling. Static analyzer flagged documentation URLs as network risk (lines 4, 22) and falsely detected weak cryptography - both are false positives. No executable code, no actual network calls, no file system operations present. Safe for publication.
valueStatement: Developers often accidentally expose secrets in AI chat sessions, terminal history, and git commits. This skill provides secure patterns to keep environment variables protected throughout your development workflow.
limitations: This skill provides guidance only - it does not automatically protect or encrypt your secrets
Does not integrate with external secrets management services like Vault or AWS Secrets Manager
Requires manual implementation of the recommended patterns by the developer
Does not scan existing codebases for accidentally committed secrets

useCases.targetUser: Backend developers building APIs with sensitive credentials
useCases.title: New Project Setup
useCases.description: Set up secure environment variable handling from the start of a new project to prevent accidental secret exposure during AI-assisted development.

useCases.targetUser: Engineering leads managing teams using Claude Code
useCases.title: Team Onboarding
useCases.description: Establish consistent secret handling practices across a development team using AI coding assistants to reduce human error risks.

useCases.title: Security Audit Remediation
useCases.description: Fix previously identified secret exposure vulnerabilities by implementing proper environment variable patterns.

promptTemplates.title: Basic .env Setup
promptTemplates.prompt: Help me create a secure .env file structure for my Node.js project. Show me what variables to include and how to set up .gitignore to prevent commits.
promptTemplates.scenario: Beginner - setting up first project with environment variables

promptTemplates.title: Safe Variable Referencing
promptTemplates.prompt: I need to debug an API connection issue without exposing my API key in the Claude chat. Show me how to reference environment variables safely when asking for help.
promptTemplates.scenario: Intermediate - working with AI assistants while protecting secrets

promptTemplates.title: Terminal Safety Patterns
promptTemplates.prompt: Review my deployment script and identify any places where environment variable values might leak to terminal history or logs. Suggest secure alternatives.
promptTemplates.scenario: Intermediate - auditing existing workflows for secret exposure

promptTemplates.title: Team Security Policy
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a checklist for my team to follow when working with secrets in AI-assisted development. Include do's and don'ts for Claude Code sessions.

bestPractices: Always add .env files to .gitignore before writing any secrets to them
Use placeholder values or redacted formats when discussing issues in AI chat sessions
Create .env.example files with descriptive placeholder values for team onboarding without sharing real secrets
antiPatterns: Never paste actual secret values, API keys, or connection strings directly into AI chat conversations
Do not use echo or console.log to debug environment variable values in terminal - this exposes them in shell history
Avoid committing .env files even temporarily with plans to delete later - git history preserves them

faq.question: Can this skill automatically protect my existing secrets?
faq.answer: No, this skill provides guidance and patterns for secure handling. You must manually implement the recommendations in your projects and workflows.

faq.question: What if I already committed secrets to git?
faq.answer: Immediately rotate those credentials as they are considered compromised. Use git filter-branch or BFG Repo-Cleaner to remove from history, then implement the patterns from this skill.

faq.question: Does this work with Docker and containerized deployments?
faq.answer: Yes, the principles apply to containers. Pass secrets via docker --env-file, Kubernetes secrets, or mounted volumes - never bake them into Dockerfiles.

faq.question: Is it safe to use environment variable names in AI chat?
faq.answer: Yes, variable names like DATABASE_URL or API_KEY are safe to reference. Only the actual values should never be shared or logged.

faq.question: How do I share environment setup with my team securely?
faq.answer: Create a .env.example file with placeholder values and documentation. Share this file in git. Team members create their own .env with real values locally.

faq.question: What should I do if Claude asks me to paste my config for debugging?
faq.answer: Replace all secret values with placeholders like *** or YOUR_VALUE_HERE before pasting. The structure and variable names are what matter for debugging, not the actual values.
